metadata.summary: Standard layout, file responsibilities, module types, and design principles for authoring self-contained, single-responsibility Terraform modules.

content.title: Terraform Module Structure and Design
content.summary: Terraform modules are self-contained packages of Terraform configurations that encapsulate reusable infrastructure components. Well-designed modules reduce code duplication, improve maintainability, and enable team collaboration at scale. Every module must have a single responsibility, a clear inputs/outputs contract, and comprehensive documentation.

why.p: Modules are the primary unit of code reuse in Terraform. Without a standard structure, teams end up with mega-modules that mix VPC, ECS, and RDS in one directory — increasing blast radius and coupling. A consistent file layout (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf) makes modules predictable: agents and engineers know exactly where to find resources, inputs, outputs, and version constraints. The single-responsibility principle further limits the surface area that changes when a dependency evolves.

list.item: Authoring a new reusable Terraform module for a specific resource type (VPC, ECS service, RDS instance).
Refactoring an existing flat Terraform root that is growing beyond a single concern.
Onboarding a new engineer or agent to an existing module — use this as the canonical layout reference.
Publishing a module to the Terraform Registry or an internal registry.
One-off scripts or environment-specific configs — a root module with inline resources is simpler and sufficient.
Prototype repos with fewer than 5 resources — the overhead of a full module structure exceeds the value.

section.name: standard-layout
section.p: The canonical module directory layout separates concerns by file. Every file has a single declared responsibility and agents must not place resources in variables.tf or outputs in main.tf.

rule.statement: Every Terraform module MUST contain at minimum: main.tf (primary resources), variables.tf (input definitions), outputs.tf (output values), and versions.tf (provider and Terraform version constraints). Optional files: locals.tf for computed values, data.tf for data source lookups.
rule.rationale: Predictable layout lets agents and engineers locate any construct in O(1) — no file scanning required. versions.tf prevents silent provider upgrades that break consumers.

example.title: Standard module layout
example.code: modules/ ├── vpc/ │ ├── main.tf # Resources │ ├── variables.tf # Input variables │ ├── outputs.tf # Output values │ ├── versions.tf # Provider requirements │ ├── locals.tf # Local values (optional) │ ├── data.tf # Data sources (optional) │ └── README.md # Documentation ├── ec2/ │ ├── main.tf │ ├── variables.tf │ └── outputs.tf └── rds/ ├── main.tf ├── variables.tf └── outputs.tf
example.note: ec2 and rds omit locals.tf and data.tf — only include optional files when they carry real content.

example.title: Mixed-responsibility module
example.code: modules/ └── app/ ├── main.tf # VPC + ECS + RDS + IAM all in one file └── variables.tf
example.note: A single module owning network, compute, and data tiers means any VPC change risks breaking the ECS service config. Split into vpc/, ecs-service/, and rds/ modules.

section.name: file-responsibilities
section.p: Each file in a module has a declared role. Deviating from this contract forces consumers to grep the entire module to understand the resource graph.

rule.statement: main.tf MUST contain only resource and module blocks. Data source blocks belong in data.tf. Local value blocks belong in locals.tf.
rule.rationale: Keeps main.tf as the authoritative resource inventory; reviewers can audit resource creation without filtering noise.

rule.statement: variables.tf MUST define every input variable with: a description, a type constraint, and a default value where the variable is optional. Sensitive inputs MUST set sensitive = true.
rule.rationale: Missing descriptions break terraform-docs automation. Missing type constraints cause runtime panics on type mismatches. Unmarked sensitive variables leak to logs.

rule.statement: outputs.tf MUST export resource IDs, ARNs, and connection strings needed by callers. Every output MUST have a description. Sensitive outputs MUST set sensitive = true.
rule.rationale: Callers cannot reference internals of a module — outputs are the only API surface. Undocumented outputs force callers to read the source.

rule.statement: versions.tf MUST declare required_version for Terraform and required_providers with source and version for every provider the module uses. Child modules MUST NOT contain provider blocks.
rule.rationale: Provider blocks in child modules cause Terraform to instantiate a duplicate provider configuration, breaking inheritance from the root module and causing apply failures.

section.name: module-types
section.p: Terraform recognizes three module roles. Understanding the distinction prevents misconfigurations (e.g., backend blocks in child modules).
section.term: The main working directory where terraform apply runs. Contains the backend block, provider configuration, and calls to child modules. Exactly one per environment.
Reusable module called by the root or by other modules. Located in modules/ directory or sourced from an external registry. MUST NOT contain backend or provider blocks.
Module shared via the Terraform Registry, GitHub, or a private registry. MUST follow the naming convention: terraform-PROVIDER-NAME (e.g., terraform-aws-vpc, terraform-google-sql).

section.name: design-principles
section.p: Four principles govern whether a module is well-designed. Violations are the most common source of module technical debt.

rule.statement: Each module MUST have a single responsibility. A module named vpc MUST NOT manage ECS services or RDS instances. A module named ecs-service MUST NOT create VPCs.
rule.rationale: Single responsibility limits the blast radius of changes. A VPC change should never require touching ECS task definitions.

rule.statement: Modules MUST avoid mega-modules. A module with more than 500 lines across all .tf files is a candidate for splitting into smaller child modules.
rule.rationale: Large modules have many more reasons to change, require longer plan times, and make it harder for reviewers to verify correctness.

rule.statement: Module dependencies MUST flow through explicit outputs and inputs. Modules MUST NOT reference each other's internal resources by hardcoded names or ARNs.
rule.rationale: Implicit coupling breaks when either module renames an internal resource. Explicit interfaces survive refactoring.

rule.statement: Modules MUST provide sensible defaults for optional variables, use validation blocks for input constraints, and add descriptions to every variable and output.
rule.rationale: Without defaults, every caller must specify every input. Without validation, invalid inputs surface as cryptic provider errors instead of readable messages.

antipattern.description: Using a single monolithic module that provisions VPC, subnets, security groups, ECS cluster, ECS services, RDS, and IAM roles in one directory.
antipattern.reason: Changes to the RDS parameter group force a full plan across all resources, making plan output unreadable. A bug in the VPC CIDR blocks can destroy the ECS service if the plan is applied carelessly.

antipattern.description: Omitting validation blocks on variables that accept CIDRs, ARNs, or instance types.
antipattern.reason: Terraform passes invalid values straight to the provider, which surfaces an obscure API error instead of a clear error_message from the validation block.

section.name: variable-output-guidelines
section.p: Variables and outputs are the public API of a module. Treat them with the same discipline as a function signature in application code.

checklist.check: All variables have descriptions (enables terraform-docs automation).
Sensible defaults provided for optional inputs (callers should not need to specify every variable).
Type constraints defined: string, number, bool, list, map, object — never omit.
Validation blocks for format-constrained inputs (CIDR, ARN, name length).
sensitive = true on any variable that holds a password, token, or key.
Complex types use object() with explicit attribute structure rather than any.
Resource IDs, ARNs, and connection strings exported as outputs.
sensitive = true on any output that echoes a secret value.

example.title: Variable with validation and description
Outputs with descriptions and sensitivity

section.name: project-layout
section.p: The module directory lives within a larger Terraform project. The recommended project layout separates environments from shared modules and global resources.

example.title: Recommended Terraform project layout
example.code: terraform/ ├── environments/ │ ├── dev/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── outputs.tf │ │ ├── terraform.tfvars │ │ └── backend.tf │ ├── staging/ │ └── prod/ ├── modules/ │ ├── vpc/ │ ├── ec2/ │ ├── rds/ │ └── ecs/ ├── global/ │ ├── iam/ │ └── dns/ └── README.md
example.note: Each environment directory is a root module. It calls child modules from ../modules/. The global/ directory holds resources shared across environments (IAM roles, Route53 zones).

rule.statement: Use .tfvars files or per-environment directories for environment differences. MUST NOT use terraform workspace as the primary environment isolation mechanism for production infrastructure.
rule.rationale: Workspaces share state backends and make it easy to accidentally apply the wrong environment. Per-environment directories provide strong isolation with separate state files and explicit variable sets.
